title: Governments are still underestimating the power of sports
gist: A former French deputy sports minister argues governments systematically underprice sports as an asset class and policy lever, and urges treating the sports ecosystem (stadiums, sponsorships, broadcast rights, franchises) as institutional-investable infrastructure—while flagging foreign-ownership national-security risks. Title IX and NWSL economics are offered as proof that targeted sports policy unlocks measurable economic and social returns.

themes.theme: Sports as an investable asset class

insights.claim: Governments should develop sports as an asset class alongside infrastructure, technology, and real estate, introducing policy to channel institutional capital into the sports ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Signals a policy tailwind for sports-linked private capital, franchise equity, and stadium/infrastructure financing in emerging and frontier markets, especially Africa.
insights.quote: governments should introduce policies that harness the sports ecosystem to attract institutional capital and develop sports as an asset class, alongside infrastructure, technology, and real estate.

insights.claim: Mass sports fandom mechanically drives demand for stadiums, transportation, housing, sponsorships, and broadcasting rights, making sports franchises a catalyst for host-country investment.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Frames event hosting as an infrastructure-and-real-estate investment thesis, not just entertainment spend—relevant to host-city capex and sponsorship/media-rights revenue pools.
insights.quote: Sports franchises, therefore, stimulate serious investment for the countries hosting them.
insights.locator: As I witnessed at the France-Senegal game

insights.claim: Foreign ownership of sports assets can create national-security implications requiring policy intervention, alongside the need for transparency, anti-corruption, and ESG standards for sports federations and investments.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Foreshadows CFIUS-style screening and ESG conditionality on cross-border sports M&A and sovereign-fund club acquisitions—an emerging regulatory/headline risk for buyers.
insights.quote: National security implications may arise from foreign ownership of sports assets, requiring policy interventions.
insights.locator: With this, however, governments should ensure

themes.theme: Policy ROI: Title IX and women's soccer

insights.claim: US high school girls' soccer participation grew from roughly 700 players in 1972 to nearly 400,000 today, an expansion attributed to Title IX of the Education Amendments Act of 1972.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Quantifies how a single policy intervention created a multi-decade participation funnel feeding a professional league—template for sizing TAM from sports-participation policy.
insights.quote: In 1972, only seven hundred girls played high school soccer across the country. But today, nearly 400,000 girls play.
insights.locator: Women’s soccer in the United States demonstrates

insights.claim: The fourteen teams of the National Women's Soccer League generated $262 million in revenue last year.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Concrete revenue anchor for valuing women's pro-sports franchises and gauging the monetization runway of a still-young league.
insights.quote: The fourteen teams across the National Women’s Soccer League, for example, generated $262 million in revenue last year.
insights.locator: That has translated to the expansion

insights.claim: Governments have historically deployed sports as a geopolitical tool that can raise tensions, citing Nazi propaganda at the 1936 Berlin Olympics and the tit-for-tat US 1980 Moscow and Soviet 1984 Los Angeles Olympic boycotts.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Reinforces that mega-events carry geopolitical risk premia—boycotts, sponsor withdrawals, and reputational exposure remain live tail risks for global brands.
insights.locator: Governments that have recognized sports’ power

insights.claim: Football has directly amplified conflict, as the 1969 World Cup qualifiers exacerbated El Salvador-Honduras tensions into a four-day war killing up to 1,500 people, and a 1990 Dinamo Zagreb-Red Star Belgrade riot is seen as a symbolic start of the Yugoslav war.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: Underscores the downside scenario of politicized sport—useful context when assessing reputational/operational risk of events in tense regions.
insights.quote: the 1969 World Cup qualifiers exacerbated already existing tensions between El Salvador and Honduras, which led to a four-day armed war
insights.locator: It is difficult to ignore the role

insights.claim: Sports policy remains chronically deprioritized, with sports ministers overlooked nationally and globally and few leaders beyond Nelson Mandela (1995 Rugby World Cup) using sport for broader policy aims.
insights.speaker: Rama Yade
insights.so_what: The author's core mispricing thesis: an underutilized policy lever implies first-mover advantage for governments and investors who institutionalize sports early.
insights.quote: I find it surprising that policymakers have not treated sports as a high priority for policy.
insights.locator: There is no human activity that brings people together
